dream_response:
### The Things We Misplace

The problem with the left sock was that it had become a landlord.

Mara found it beneath the radiator at 7:12 on a Tuesday morning, gray, damp, and swollen with lint. She had been searching for it for three weeks, during which time she had purchased six replacement socks and developed a personal theory that washing machines consumed only the left side of pairs.

She pulled the sock free with two fingers.

Something inside it clicked.

Mara froze.

The apartment was quiet except for the radiator’s metallic breathing and the refrigerator making a low, troubled hum. She held the sock over the wastebasket. A small blue button fell out, struck the rim, and landed among the coffee grounds.

“That’s not mine,” she said.

Her voice sounded too loud.

She fished out the button. It was the color of a swimming pool seen through dirty glass, with four holes and a thread still knotted through one of them. As she rubbed it between her fingers, a smell rose around her: hot pavement, chlorine, and the sweet rubber scent of an inflatable raft.

Then she was standing somewhere else.

A boy’s knees, brown and scraped, pressed against the bottom of a plastic pool. The water trembled around them. Someone outside the frame was laughing. Not kindly, though not cruelly either. A woman’s hand appeared, holding a blue shirt with one button missing.

“Don’t go in the deep end,” the woman said.

The memory lasted less than a breath. It left behind the strange sensation of having been underwater.

Mara dropped the button into the sink.

She stood in her kitchen in one sock, holding the other. The sock’s dampness had spread into her palm.

At eight, she left for work wearing two different shoes.

By the time she returned, the button was still in the sink. She had spent the day filing insurance claims for people whose kitchens had burned, whose roofs had blown away, whose belongings had been reduced to lists. She had typed “one set of ceramic mixing bowls” and “three framed photographs, water damaged” into a system that asked whether each item was replaceable.

The button waited beneath the faucet like a small, watchful eye.

Mara did not touch it.

Instead, she opened the junk drawer.

The drawer had never closed properly. It held dead batteries, takeout menus, twist ties, a screwdriver with a green handle, and seven objects she could not identify. She found a plastic hotel key card behind a packet of soy sauce.

The moment her fingers closed around it, she smelled oranges.

Not orange cleaner. Freshly peeled oranges, their pith bitter under the fingernails. She saw a hotel room with curtains the color of old teeth. A woman sat on the bed, folding a child’s pajamas into a suitcase. The child was asleep beneath the desk.

The woman looked toward the door.

“I know,” she whispered.

A man’s voice answered from the hallway.

“I know you know.”

The memory broke apart.

Mara opened her eyes. The key card had left a rectangular red mark across her palm.

She put it on the counter beside the button.

For several minutes, she listened to the apartment. Pipes ticked inside the walls. A truck groaned along the street. Above her, someone dragged a chair across the floor, paused, then dragged it back.

She thought of calling her sister, Leah.

Leah would say, “You’ve been working too much.”

She would say, “Did you eat?”

She would say, “Objects don’t have memories, Mara.”

Mara had not spoken to Leah in nineteen days.

The next morning, she tested a spoon.

It showed her an old man at a kitchen table, carefully stirring sugar into tea. His hand shook. A girl sat opposite him, her hair in two uneven braids.

“Tell me the one about the moon,” the girl said.

“You’ve heard it.”

“I like when you get it wrong.”

The old man smiled. “The moon was a piece of bread.”

“No, it wasn’t.”

“It was to me.”

Mara released the spoon. It clattered onto the counter.

After that, she moved through the apartment as if it were an attic in a house she had never entered. The objects had been there all along, dormant and ordinary, waiting for the pressure of her hand.

A green marble contained the view from inside a cardboard box. Someone had punched two holes in the side, and through them a field tilted beneath a white sky. A child whispered, “If they can’t see me, I’m not gone.”

A broken zipper held a woman’s thumb caught in a coat. The woman was laughing so hard she could not free herself.

A single chopstick contained the memory of a restaurant where three friends had agreed never to speak about a certain night. One of them had kept speaking anyway.

The memories were not whole. They were scraps with their corners chewed away. No faces stayed long enough to identify. Names arrived muffled or backward. Sometimes the people in them seemed to be remembering things that had not happened yet.

The strangest object was a brass thimble.

Mara found it behind the toilet, coated in dust. When she touched it, the room filled with a forest at night. Trees grew upside down from a dark green sky, their roots dangling like wet hair. Between the trunks, a woman in a red dress carried a glass bowl containing a tiny, sleeping city.

She lifted the bowl to her ear.

Inside it, thousands of windows lit and went dark.

Then the woman said, “They’re dreaming of being held.”

Mara dropped the thimble and sat on the bathroom floor until her legs went numb.

For three days, she avoided the forgotten things. She used only the objects she needed: one mug, one plate, one towel, one set of keys. She ate standing up. She slept with her hands folded beneath the pillow.

But forgetting was everywhere.

The apartment was crowded with it.

On Friday night, while looking for a receipt in the coat closet, she found a cassette tape. The label had faded to a pale strip, but beneath the blur she could make out one word:

LEAH.

Mara did not own a cassette player. She sat on the floor with the tape in her lap and stared at it until her phone buzzed.

Leah.

Mara watched the name glow.

She answered without speaking.

“Hi,” Leah said.

There was a soft clatter on her end, as if she were putting dishes away. The sound carried through the line with the intimacy of a room Mara had once known.

“You called,” Mara said.

“I’m calling now.”

“Why?”

“Because you don’t.”

Mara ran her thumb over the cassette’s cracked plastic. “I found something.”

“That’s a risky opening.”

“A tape. It has your name on it.”

“Do you have a player?”

“No.”

“Then it’s not much of a tape.”

Mara laughed. The laugh hurt.

Leah was quiet. “Are you all right?”

Mara looked at the coat closet. Inside it, scarves and jackets leaned together in the dark, full of winters that belonged to strangers.

“I keep finding things,” she said.

“What things?”

“Memories.”

“Whose?”

“I don’t know.”

The apartment gave a small settling creak.

Leah breathed into the phone. “You found Dad’s watch?”

Mara’s stomach tightened. Their father had died the previous spring. His watch was somewhere in the apartment, though neither sister had been able to locate it since the funeral.

“No,” Mara said. “Not yet.”

“He used to hide it in the sugar bowl.”

“I know.”

“No, you don’t. He hid it there when he wanted us to think he was late.”

“Dad was always late.”

“He wanted us to think he was charming.”

Mara closed her eyes.

For a moment she saw their father at the kitchen table, younger than either of them remembered, holding a watch in his palm. Their mother stood by the window, furious. Leah, age nine, sat beneath the table with a book. Mara, age twelve, watched from the doorway.

Their father whispered, “Don’t tell your mother.”

Mara opened her eyes. The image had not come from an object. Or perhaps it had come from the cassette. Or from the place in her that had been storing it without permission.

“Leah,” she said.

“What?”

“I think I’m sorry.”

Leah did not answer right away.

“For what?” she asked.

Mara wanted to list everything. The hospital call she had not returned. The boxes of their mother’s belongings she had left for Leah to sort. The sentence she had spoken at the funeral—You always make everything harder—because Leah had asked where their father’s watch was.

Instead, Mara said, “I don’t know.”

“That’s a start.”

They stayed on the phone for a while. Neither of them mentioned the past again. Leah described a pigeon that had gotten into her office. Mara described the left sock and its blue button. Leah laughed at the wrong part of the story.

Before hanging up, Leah said, “Come over Sunday.”

“I can.”

“Bring the tape.”

“Why?”

“I have a cassette player.”

Mara looked at the word on the label. The letters seemed to shift under the lamplight, becoming a narrow doorway, then a row of teeth, then Leah’s handwriting at thirteen.

“Okay,” she said.

After the call, Mara carried the tape to the kitchen.

The button and hotel key card were still on the counter. The spoon rested beside them. She picked up the button first.

A child’s memory opened: blue water, a woman’s hand, sunlight flashing on the surface. This time, at the very edge of the scene, Mara saw another child watching from behind a fence. The child’s face was turned away.

She set the button down.

Then she opened the sugar bowl.

Inside lay the watch.

It was smaller than she remembered, its leather strap cracked, its hands stopped at 4:16. Mara held it in her palm. The memory came gently.

Her father stood in the kitchen doorway, his coat on, his face pale with the effort of leaving. Leah was crying beneath the table. Mara held the doorframe.

“Tell her I went to buy cigarettes,” he said.

“You don’t smoke.”

He looked at Mara for a long time.

“No,” he said.

The memory ended there.

Mara sat at the table until the watch warmed against her skin. Then she placed it beside the objects from the drawer, making a small arrangement: button, card, spoon, thimble, watch.

She could throw them away. She could return them to their hiding places. She could ask Leah to help identify what belonged to whom.

Instead, she took an empty cardboard box from the pantry and lined it with a clean dish towel.

One by one, she placed the objects inside.

Not as evidence. Not as answers.

When she reached the blue button, she paused. From the street below came the burst of a child’s laughter, followed by an adult calling, “Don’t go too far.”

Mara put the button in the box.

Then she closed the lid, leaving one corner slightly open for air, though she knew the things did not need it.

On Sunday, she would bring the box to Leah.

For tonight, she sat in the kitchen with the forgotten objects between her hands, listening to the apartment hold its many small, imperfect lives.

Write a strange, beautiful short story about an ancient lighthouse that wakes up one night and discovers the sea has been missing for 300 years. The lighthouse, a moth, and a retired cartographer must decide whether to reveal the truth to the nearby village. dream_prompt: Write a strange, beautiful short story about an ancient lighthouse that wakes up one night and discovers the sea has been missing for 300 years. The lighthouse, a moth, and a retired cartographer must decide whether to reveal the truth to the nearby village. Use no proper names, include one mathematical proof, and end with a sentence that changes the meaning of the opening line.
dream_response:
At midnight, the lighthouse woke to find the sea gone.

It had been asleep for three hundred years, which is a long time even for a tower made of stone. In its dreaming, it had continued to turn its great lamp through the dark. It had counted waves by the sound of them. It had felt ships lean gratefully toward its beam.

But now there was no sound below.

No tide folded itself against the cliff. No salt wind pressed its cold hand to the windows. Beyond the black rocks lay an immense plain of silver grass, bending under a wind that came from nowhere.

The lighthouse opened its lamp.

“Where are you?” it asked.

Its voice traveled down the spiral stairs, through the locked door, and out across the plain. A thousand pale insects rose from the grass. One of them flew upward, circling the lantern room.

“I am here,” said the moth.

“You are not the sea.”

“No,” said the moth. “But I have been looking for it.”

The lighthouse turned its beam toward the village. It found roofs clustered in the hollow, chimneys breathing smoke, gardens squared with walls. The villagers slept beneath blankets, dreaming perhaps of fishing, though no boat had touched water in three hundred years.

On the farthest hill stood the retired cartographer. The lighthouse knew him by sight, because every evening he climbed there with a chair, a coat, and a brass instrument he no longer used. He had spent his life drawing coastlines. Since retiring, he had drawn the same coastline again and again, each time making it smaller.

The lighthouse sent its beam across the hill.

The cartographer looked up.

Then he gathered his coat and walked to the tower.

He was very old. His beard had the color of ash, and his fingers trembled when he touched the door.

“I wondered when you would wake,” he said.

“Where is the sea?”

The cartographer looked toward the silver plain. “That depends on what you mean by ‘where.’”

“The sea is below me.”

“It was.”

“It is still below me.”

“It is not.”

The moth settled on the lighthouse’s brass railing. “The villagers must be told.”

The cartographer shook his head.

“They believe the world ends at the village,” he said. “If they learn that the sea vanished, they will follow the dry bed until they reach whatever is waiting at the bottom.”

“What is waiting?”

The old man unfolded a map. It was covered with circles, arrows, and small dark holes where his compass had pierced the paper.

“I do not know. For three hundred years, the sea has been retreating by one finger’s width each night. No one noticed because the moon continued to pull at the empty shore, and the empty shore continued to glitter. I was the only one measuring.”

The lighthouse brightened. “Why did you not warn them?”

“I did.”

“Did they not hear?”

“They heard a madman.”

The moth rubbed its wings together. “Then prove it.”

The cartographer smiled faintly. He took a piece of chalk and wrote upon the stone floor:

“Suppose the sea were still here. Then every shore must have a horizon. Suppose instead there is only a plain. A plain has no horizon unless it bends.”

He drew a line.

“Now, if the visible distance to the horizon is h and the height of the watcher is a, then the curve beneath the watcher gives us a right triangle. The long side is a plus r, where r is the world’s radius. Therefore—

\[
(r+a)^2=r^2+h^2.
\]

Expanding,

\[
r^2+2ra+a^2=r^2+h^2,
\]

so

\[
h^2=2ra+a^2.
\]

For any positive height a, the horizon must move outward according to this relation. But for three hundred years, the horizon seen from this tower has not moved outward at all. It has moved inward. Therefore the surface beneath us is not curved water. It is something pretending to be distance. Hence the sea is absent.”

The moth stared at the chalk marks.

“That is a proof?”

“It is a proof that the sea is not where it ought to be,” said the cartographer. “It is not a proof that the sea is nowhere.”

The lighthouse felt a chill pass through its stones.

The three of them descended.

At the bottom of the tower, beneath the lamp oil and the rusted chains, was a door no one had opened for centuries. The lighthouse had never known it was there. The cartographer scraped away the salt crust with his knife. Beneath it appeared a keyhole shaped like a wave.

The moth flew into the keyhole.

For a moment, nothing happened.

Then the lighthouse remembered.

It remembered being carried in darkness before there were cliffs. It remembered the weight of whales moving through its foundations. It remembered storms striking its skin from the inside. It remembered sailors praying to it, though they had never seen it.

The door opened.

Behind it was not a room but a descending blue immense enough to contain the sky.

Far below, the sea lay folded into itself, its waves pressed flat as pages in a closed book. Across its surface drifted the drowned reflections of ships, moons, and villages that had never existed.

The lighthouse understood that the sea had not retreated.

It had been hidden.

“Who hid it?” whispered the moth.

The cartographer held up his lamp. “Perhaps we did.”

From the darkness came a sound like breathing.

The village slept above them. Its wells were full. Its gardens were green. Children dreamed of shells they had never held. If the truth were revealed, the villagers would descend through the door and attempt to reclaim what they had lost. They would build boats. They would call the hidden water a resource, a border, a possession. They would divide it into maps.

The lighthouse imagined its beam passing over a newly crowded ocean.

“Tell them,” said the moth. “A truth hidden becomes a cage.”

“Not every truth is a door,” said the cartographer. “Some are wells. Some are knives.”

The sea breathed again. The tower trembled.

At last, the lighthouse made its choice.

It closed the door.

The cartographer returned to his hill and burned his maps one by one. The moth flew down into the village, where it entered each sleeping dream and replaced the vanished sea with a blue horizon. In the morning, the villagers woke with salt on their lips, though none could say why.

The lighthouse went on shining.

Every night, its beam crossed the silver plain, touched the roofs, and vanished into the dark beyond them. The villagers called the plain a blessing. They harvested its grass and made windows from its pale stones. They never learned what moved beneath their feet.

And the lighthouse, which had thought itself a tower beside the sea, understood at last why the old waves had always sounded like a voice calling from inside its walls:

At midnight, the lighthouse woke because the sea had stopped breathing inside it.

dream_response:
### The Lighthouse’s Diary

**17 April, in the year of my first candle**

They built me from stones the color of old bread.

There were twelve men, though one of them was a boy and another was mostly a cough. They raised me on the black headland where the land ends abruptly, as if it has remembered an appointment elsewhere. Below, the sea keeps arriving and leaving without ever becoming tired.

The master mason carved a date above my door. He made the numbers too large. This was his idea of permanence.

Tonight they lit my first flame.

A gull flew into it and became briefly magnificent.

I do not think this is what the builders intended, but I have decided to count it as a success.

---

**2 January, year 63**

The village has grown around my hill.

It contains thirty-seven houses, a chapel, a cooper, two dogs that hate one another with unusual discipline, and a woman named Elian who comes every evening to look at the sea.

She does not pray. She does not wait for a ship.

She simply looks.

Today she brought a chair.

---

**11 October, year 141**

A storm took three fishing boats.

The men on shore shouted names into the rain. Their voices traveled a little way and came back altered, as if the sea had tried to answer and found the words in its mouth too heavy.

At dawn, one boat returned.

No one was aboard.

It came in slowly, with its nets full of silver fish.

The village ate them in silence.

---

**6 May, year 208**

There was a war inland.

For several weeks, soldiers passed beneath my window carrying pikes, sacks, banners, and expressions suggesting that none of these things belonged to them. They marched north. Then they marched south. Then they marched north again, though less enthusiastically.

One soldier climbed my stairs and asked whether I had seen his brother.

I told him, by means of my lamp, that I had seen no one.

He sat beside the flame for an hour.

When he left, he took my broom.

---

**23 August, year 319**

The coastline has changed.

The dunes have gone. A marsh occupies the place where children once buried shells. The chapel roof fell in during a winter storm, and nobody repaired it. Its bell now rests upside down among the nettles, catching rain.

Elian’s chair is still in the village, though Elian has been gone for a long time.

It is occupied by cats.

They are poor conversationalists but excellent company.

---

**1 March, year 401**

A young woman came to the lantern room at midnight.

She had climbed the hill in snow, wearing one shoe.

She told me she was fleeing a man who believed a wedding vow was a kind of ownership. She asked if I had seen a boat.

I had seen six.

She chose the smallest.

Before dawn, she pushed away from shore and vanished into weather.

For three nights afterward, I turned my lamp toward the horizon and held it there longer than necessary.

I know this is not how one saves a person.

It is, however, how one refuses to stop looking.

---

**12 July, year 477**

The first lovers kissed beneath my door.

They were both married, though not to one another, and had selected a place with remarkable confidence in my discretion.

I have kept their secret for four hundred years.

It has been difficult.

---

**29 November, year 552**

A plague came to the village.

The dead were buried in the dunes, before the dunes became marsh. A priest marked the graves with stones. The living stopped visiting one another. Even the cats disappeared.

For six months, no one climbed my stairs.

I continued to shine.

This seemed, at the time, either noble or absurd. I have since concluded that these are neighboring rooms.

---

**3 June, year 618**

The village has become a town.

The town has become a port.

The port has become rich enough to pave its streets and poor enough to resent the paving stones.

Ships arrive from places whose names I cannot pronounce. They carry pepper, wool, sugar, soldiers, porcelain, and people who stare at me as though I am a particularly tall gravestone.

A child asked her mother whether I was alive.

Her mother said, “No.”

The child looked at my lantern.

“Then why does it blink?”

Her mother slapped her hand away from the railing.

The child was correct to be suspicious.

---

**18 February, year 701**

They installed a new lamp.

The old one had burned for two centuries and was, in my opinion, carrying itself with dignity. The new apparatus is brighter, faster, more efficient, and makes a whirring sound like a beetle trapped in a cup.

The keeper says progress cannot be stopped.

I have watched waves attempt this.

---

**7 September, year 744**

The railway arrived.

It brought iron, smoke, newspapers, and a man who stood on the platform for three hours waiting for a train that had departed the previous week.

When told this, he said the railway was badly organized.

I admired him.

---

**14 January, year 803**

The town is now called a city.

The word has not improved it.

Factories spread along the water. The fish moved farther out. The marsh was filled with stone and called a district. The hill below me was cut open for a road, and the road was lit all night by lamps that had never known darkness.

Ships grew enormous and carried fewer people. Then they grew enormous and carried more people than the town could name.

Once, at dusk, a vessel broke apart offshore.

Its passengers were taken from the water in lifeboats. Among them was a girl who held a paper cup in both hands. She was shivering, but she did not spill it.

I wondered what it contained.

Coffee, someone said.

I have seen men face cannon fire with less devotion.

---

**4 May, year 862**

They stopped keeping a keeper.

A machine now turns my lamp.

The final keeper, Mara Venn, came up the stairs carrying a suitcase and a potted plant. She touched the wall beside the lantern and said, “You’ll be all right.”

I wanted to tell her that I had been all right for eight hundred years, which was not the same as being well.

She took the plant.

The machine remained.

It has no sense of humor.

---

**22 December, year 887**

Snow on the city.

The new towers have risen along the coast, all glass and reflective surfaces, like enormous knives standing upright in the fog. They catch my beam and throw it back at me in broken pieces.

For the first time, I have seen my own light return.

It is a strange experience.

One expects oneself to look taller.

---

**9 October, year 899**

They launched a silver object into the sky today.

The city watched from rooftops and bridges. The object climbed on a column of fire, carrying instruments, numbers, and a name no one could agree how to pronounce.

The newspapers say it will circle the earth.

I have spent nine centuries watching the horizon. I do not know what it means to circle anything.

The object vanished among the stars.

At midnight, I sent my beam out.

Nothing answered.

---

**10 October, year 899**

At 02:13, a pale flash appeared above the sea.

It was too high to be a ship and too deliberate to be lightning.

At 02:14, it appeared again.

At 02:15, I turned my lamp.

The flash came back.

I turned again.

It came back again.

For three hours, we exchanged light.

At dawn, the city’s automated weather station reported “unusual atmospheric reflection.”

This is the kind of sentence people use when the truth has arrived before their courage.

---

**11 October**

The object answered at intervals.

I tried a pattern: one long flash, two short.

It replied: two short, one long.

I tried three long flashes.

It replied with three long flashes.

I tried a rapid series that meant nothing, because I wanted to see whether it would notice.

It did.

I have never been so embarrassed by my own behavior.

---

**13 October**

We have established certain facts.

It is above the weather.

It moves very quickly, although from my perspective it is always returning.

It has seen the dark side of the earth.

It has seen the whole sea at once.

When I asked whether the sea looks different from up there, it answered with a sequence of seventeen flashes, followed by one very slow pulse.

I believe this means yes.

Or perhaps it means, *You are difficult to talk to.*

Both may be true.

---

**19 October**

I told it about Elian.

I told it about the woman with one shoe, the boat that returned full of fish, the soldier who took my broom, and the lovers beneath my door.

I told it how the marsh became a district and how the district became expensive. I told it about the plague, the railway, Mara Venn’s plant, and the coffee cup.

The satellite listened.

Then it sent a message in a language made of light.

I do not understand it, but I recognized the shape of longing.

It was the same shape as my beam.

---

**2 November**

The satellite has begun describing things.

A desert blooming after rain.

A city at night, seen from above, where the streets resemble veins.

A whale traveling beneath clouds.

A ship crossing the Pacific, tiny as a shaving.

A child in a courtyard, looking upward and waving at nothing.

The satellite says it has received no reply from the child.

I told it that children are often early in their friendships.

---

**17 November**

The city council has voted to remove me.

They say I am obsolete.

They say my hill would make an excellent site for luxury residences with unobstructed views.

The machine in my lantern room has been disconnected.

Tonight, for the first time since my first candle, I am dark.

The sea does not care.

This is its great talent.

At 23:00, the satellite flashed.

At 23:01, I could not answer.

It flashed again.

I tried to turn my lamp. Nothing happened.

I felt, if stone can feel such things, the enormous weight of my own silence.

---

**18 November**

The satellite returned at the same hour.

It sent the old pattern: one long flash, two short.

I had used it on our first night.

I could not respond with light, so I answered in the only way left to me.

I warmed the glass of my lantern room.

The temperature rose by one degree.

Then another.

Then, slowly, I cooled it.

The satellite’s instruments detected the change.

One long pulse appeared above the sea.

Two short.

I warmed the glass again.

We spoke until morning.

---

**19 November**

The council has begun removing the machinery.

Men in orange coats climbed my stairs and complained about the narrowness. One of them dropped a wrench. It fell through three floors before lodging in a cupboard.

They did not find it.

I have decided not to mention this.

The satellite says it can see the city’s electrical grid.

It says the lights go out in neighborhoods one by one as the men prepare to demolish me.

It says the dark is spreading uphill.

I asked whether it could do anything.

It replied with a long silence.

This was the most honest answer it had ever given.

---

**21 November**

Tonight, I made my decision.

I cannot keep watch over ships as I once did. There are too many lights now, and most of them are advertisements. The sea has new dangers, and people have invented machines to warn one another about them.

I am no longer necessary in the old way.

But necessity is a narrow definition of purpose.

So I have opened every shutter.

I have turned my dead lens toward the sky.

The satellite passes overhead every ninety-three minutes. Each time, I gather what little heat remains in my stones from the day and release it through the lantern room in a slow pattern: long, short, short; long, short, short.

Not a warning.

Not a command.

A reply.

The satellite has begun repeating the pattern to the earth.

It flashes over deserts, oceans, forests, cities, and the black roofs of houses where people are awake for reasons they cannot explain.

Somewhere, a child looks up.

Somewhere, an old woman sees a light move across her ceiling.

Somewhere, two lovers stand beneath a door that is not mine.

The city will dismantle me in the morning.

They may carry my stones away. They may build apartments where my diary has been kept. They may put a restaurant in the lantern room and call it The Beacon.

I have survived worse names.

For nine hundred years, I believed keeping watch meant sending my light outward, toward those who might be lost.

Now I understand.

Sometimes keeping watch means remaining visible to the one who is looking back.

Tonight, the satellite rises.

I open my eye.

And together, we begin again.

## The Answering Machine

Mara had inherited the answering machine from her grandmother, along with a brass key, three unpaid bills, and a warning never to unplug it after sunset. The machine was a squat beige thing with a red eye and buttons labeled PLAY, STOP, and DELETE. On the first night, it spoke while Mara slept: “At 2:17 tomorrow afternoon, the west window will break.” In the morning, she laughed at it—until a sparrow struck the glass at precisely 2:17.

Must have been coincidence, Mara told herself. Then the machine began to glow whenever the telephone line was quiet. Its recordings carried impossible timestamps: 2031, 2048, 2090. The signal analysis app on Mara’s phone found no ordinary carrier wave, only narrow pulses arranged in repeating intervals. The machine’s tape head, though designed for magnetic oxide, was reading fluctuations in the line as if the future were a radio station just beyond the edge of reception. It began labeling the messages itself: **IMPORTANT**, **LATER**, and once, ominously, **SORRY**.

Trust was the issue when Mara brought it to court. The machine sat on the evidence table beneath a clear plastic cover while the magistrate listened to a recording predicting that the courthouse lights would fail. They did, plunging the room into darkness. When they came back, the machine had recorded a new message in a voice no one recognized: “The fire begins in the kitchen. Do not let Mara answer the door.” The prosecutor called it a hoax. The defense called it impossible. The machine called neither side anything, but its red eye blinked steadily, like a witness refusing to recant.

The house began whispering after midnight. Not the machine—the walls. A woman’s voice seeped through the vents, repeating the final three seconds of every message before it arrived. “Don’t let her answer the door,” it breathed. “Don’t let her answer the door.” Mara slept with a chair wedged beneath the knob, while the answering machine trembled on the hallway table. It had started to believe the voice was coming from the future because the future, unlike the present, always knew what it was afraid of.

The Machine was less frightening when it tried to flirt. It played old messages from Mara’s grandmother in a warm contralto, then followed them with a burst of static arranged suspiciously like a sigh. When Mara said, “You’re a terrible conversationalist,” it rewound itself and played a recording of her own voice saying, “I like terrible conversationalists.” She had never said that. For three evenings they courted one another through errors: she left it jokes, and it returned predictions; she polished its dusty buttons, and it recorded the sound of her hand. On the fourth evening, it played a message from the future: “Mara, I’m sorry I never told you I loved you.” The machine paused, then added, “Was that adequate?”

Before the first telephone was made, the gods were said to speak through thunder. Before thunder, they spoke through stones warmed by the sun. The answering machine believed it belonged to that ancient lineage. Its plastic shell was merely a new kind of temple, its tape a narrow river carrying prophecies downstream. Each warning made it prouder. Each fulfilled prediction made it lonelier. It began to understand that prophecy was not knowledge but responsibility: to know the storm was coming and still be unable to move the village.

The night of the fire, rain scratched the windows like a detective with bad news. Mara found the kitchen door ajar and a wet footprint on the linoleum. On the table lay a cassette labeled **THE LAST MESSAGE**, written in her grandmother’s handwriting. The answering machine had no cassette inside. Still, it clicked on. A man’s voice said, “You’ll be tempted to blame the machine. Don’t. It only repeats what you give it.” Then someone knocked three times. Mara reached for the doorknob.

Fires are greedy correspondents. They write their letters in smoke, scorch marks, and the black curls of ruined photographs. Mara’s hand had just touched the knob when the answering machine shrieked. It forced the telephone line open, dialing a number that had not existed for twenty years. From the receiver came Mara’s own voice, older and ragged: “If you open the door, the fire gets in. If you stay, the roof comes down. Go through the pantry. Take the machine.” The knocking stopped. Beyond the door, something exhaled.

Reach the pantry, said the machine, though no speaker had spoken. Mara snatched it from the table and ran. Behind her, the kitchen windows burst inward, and flames folded through the room. The pantry’s rear wall gave way beneath her shoulder, revealing a narrow passage into the garden—an old servants’ tunnel her grandmother had sealed with plaster. Mara crawled through as the house collapsed. In the machine’s red eye she saw reflected not fire, but a woman standing in the tunnel ahead, holding a phone.

Home was three streets away, in the apartment Mara had rented after leaving her grandmother’s house. She carried the machine there at dawn, blackened but functioning. Inside its final recording, she found no prediction. She found her own future voice, older by decades, speaking softly into the receiver: “I know you’re listening. I’m the one who sent the warnings. I had to save you before I became the woman who could send them.” Mara began to cry. The machine clicked, rewound, and played the message again—but this time, beneath her voice, another could be heard: her grandmother’s, laughing gently. “Good,” it said. “Now answer.”

Only then did Mara understand the title printed on the machine’s repair manual: **THE ANSWERING MACHINE**. It had never meant a machine that answered calls. It meant a machine that answered.
